modules.title: 6-Block System
modules.subtitle: Meta, Input, Output, Reasoning, Quality, Advanced
modules.description: Six prompt structure blocks in one clear course – the full system from A to Z.

slides.title: Same task. Two different answers.
slides.subtitle: Why does AI sometimes answer brilliantly and sometimes vaguely?

content.whyBenefit: After this module you'll write prompts 6x better than before.
content.heroSubText: The problem isn't AI. The problem is your prompt.
content.structuredPrompt:
META: You are a marketing strategist with 10 years of B2B experience.
Goal: prepare a Q1 marketing plan.
Audience: CMO and leadership.

INPUT: Budget 50k EUR. Target market: Baltic countries.
Competitors: 3 main players.

OUTPUT: Strategic document, 2–3 pages.
Structure: goals → channels → budget → key indicators.
Tone: professional, with concrete numbers.
content.aboutText: In this course you'll learn the 6-block system – from a messy question to a professional result.
content.sandboxMessage: This is a sandbox – you can try, make mistakes, and go back. Results aren't saved here; what matters is learning.
outcomes: Write prompts that give predictable results
Use the 6-block structure in any AI tool
Save time – fewer iterations, more precision
content.toolsIntro: The 6-block principles work in any of these tools. Below – a short intro and the most common use cases.

tools.name: ChatGPT (OpenAI)
tools.description: General-purpose conversational AI – best for everyday writing, summaries, and idea generation.

tools.name: Claude (Anthropic)
tools.description: Strong on long texts and documents; careful, consistent response style.

tools.name: Gemini (Google)
tools.description: Integrated with Google services – mail, docs, search in one place.

tools.name: Copilot (Microsoft)
tools.description: In the Microsoft stack – Outlook, Word, Teams; great for office work.

tools.name: Grok (xAI)
tools.description: Real-time data from X (Twitter) – news, trends, public discussion.

tools.name: DeepSeek
tools.description: Strong for coding, maths, and logic – good value for money.

content.footer: Next – slide 3: Prompt and prompt engineering

slides.title: Prompt and prompt engineering
slides.subtitle: Two terms that change how well you work with AI

content.contextIntro: Every time you write to AI, you're writing a prompt. The question is: do you do it in a structured way?
content.promptDefinition: A prompt is a text instruction to an AI model. Every question, task, or direction you give the AI is a prompt.
content.engineeringDefinition: Prompt engineering is a systematic approach to what and how you write to AI. Not guesswork – a process: specification, structure, iteration.

aspects.title: Specification
aspects.description: Clear definition of the task: goal, audience, requirements, and constraints.
aspects.example: Goal: prepare a Q3 sales summary for the board. Audience: 6 members. Constraints: 1 page, English.

aspects.title: Structure
aspects.description: How you organise information: context, data, format, priorities.
aspects.example: Separate and number: 1) META 2) INPUT 3) OUTPUT 4) QUALITY. Briefly – what to write.

aspects.title: Iteration
aspects.description: Try, measure, refine, and check quality until the result fits your needs.
aspects.example: Iteration: 1) format 2) key indicators 3) criteria. Briefly – what to check.

slides.title: Workflow concept
slides.subtitle: From simple chat to work results

content.intro: Chat = free-form answer. Workflow = structured result.

diagrams.title: 1) Chat (Basic)
diagrams.subtitle: Good for ideas, explanations, discussion.
diagrams.note: Limited control – AI guesses what you want.

diagrams.title: 2) Workflow
diagrams.subtitle: Good for documents, analysis, plans.
diagrams.note: AI becomes a work tool, not just a chat.

examples.title: Workflow
examples.prompt:
You are a training designer.
Create a 5-point explanation of prompt engineering.
Format: numbered list.
Tone: professional.
Max 120 words.

slides.title: Main prompt types
slides.subtitle: Three core types for effective communication with AI

types.name: System prompts
types.description: Set the AI's goal and boundaries.
types.example: You are a marketing analyst; provide the structure and key metrics for a competitor analysis, please.
types.result: AI acts as a specialist analyst, giving structured business insights.

types.name: Context prompts
types.description: Give the task and important context.
types.example: Prepare a competitor analysis for the logistics sector, short and clear, using 2024 data.
types.result: Answers focused on the market and the latest data.

types.name: Role prompts
types.example: Imagine you're a company lead presenting the strategy to the team – short and convincing.
types.result: Text in a leader's voice – inspiring, forward-looking, motivating.

content.practicalTip: Combine all three prompt types in one request to get the most precise, business-ready result. Especially important for strategy documents or analysis.

slides.title: Prompting techniques
slides.subtitle: 6 basic techniques and copyable examples

techniques.title: Zero-shot
techniques.description: One clear request with no extra examples.

techniques.title: Few-shot
techniques.description: Give 1–2 examples and ask for new ones.
techniques.example: Examples: "Less stress, more energy." "Your health – your plan." Create 3 more.

techniques.title: Chain of thought
techniques.description: Break the task into clear steps.

techniques.title: Prompt chain
techniques.description: Use one result as input for the next.
techniques.example: Create outline → Draft plan → Write intro.

techniques.title: Instruction
techniques.example: Write a 200-word message, formal tone, in English.

techniques.title: Manipulation (avoid)
techniques.example: Prove that X is better than Y.

slides.title: Good prompt template
slides.subtitle: META + INPUT + OUTPUT

blocks.title: META (role + goal + audience)
blocks.description: Who you are, what you're doing, and who the result is for.
blocks.example: Role – marketing lead. Goal – SWOT analysis. Audience – leadership.

blocks.title: INPUT (data + constraints)
blocks.description: Facts, numbers, and limits you're working with.

blocks.title: OUTPUT (format + structure + tone)
blocks.description: How the result should look and sound.
blocks.example: Format – table. Structure – S/W/O/T, 3 points each. Tone – professional.

content.template:
META: Role – [who you are]. Goal – [what to create]. Audience – [who for].
INPUT: Data – [facts/numbers]. Constraints – [time/budget].
OUTPUT: Format – [table/list/document]. Structure – [points/sections]. Tone – [style].
content.example:
META: Role – marketing lead. Goal – prepare SWOT analysis. Audience – leadership.
INPUT: Data – product "EcoBox", goal retail. Constraints – 1 page.
OUTPUT: Format – table with bullets. Structure – S/W/O/T, 3 points each. Tone – professional, clear.
content.footer: Next – slide 8: What you've learned so far

slides.title: What you've learned so far
slides.subtitle: From a clear task to validation

content.title: So far – clear task. Next – control.

mapping.to: You have a clear task with META, INPUT, OUTPUT.
Add Reasoning, Quality, and Advanced – for checking and consistency.

content.note: Complex tasks need more than clarity – they need validation.
content.takeaway: From 3 blocks to 6 blocks = from task to control.

slides.title: Hierarchical structure
slides.subtitle: 6-block system: from most to least critical

content.introBody: AI models read and process information in order. The most important information should come first so the result matches your expectations.
content.tip: Try creating a prompt without structure (as you usually would). Save it – we'll compare with the structured version at the end.

slides.title: 1 Meta block
slides.subtitle: Role, context, and goal – who are you and what do you do?
slides.keyQuestion: Who are you and what do you do?

slides.title: 2 Input block
slides.subtitle: Facts, data, and constraints – what do you have?
slides.keyQuestion: What data and constraints do you have?

slides.title: 3 Output block
slides.subtitle: Format and structure – what do you expect?
slides.keyQuestion: What format and structure do you want?

slides.title: Reasoning models
slides.subtitle: CoT vs ToT – how is AI reasoning controlled?

veiksmoIntro.trumpai: **Benefit:** Two reasoning models – chain (CoT) and tree (ToT). Chain = sequence. Tree = alternatives. Choice depends on the task.
veiksmoIntro.darykDabar: **What to do:** Review both models below. Pick one template (CoT or ToT), copy it, and run it in AI with your business task.
veiksmoIntro.patikra: Did AI follow the reasoning sequence? If the answer is too shallow – try the other model.

sections.heading: Why it works
sections.body: AI doesn't "think" on its own like a person – it follows your instructions. When you specify a reasoning sequence (CoT or ToT), the model outputs step by step or compares options by your rules. So the right model choice and clear structure in the prompt lead to better, more predictable results.

slides.title: 4 Reasoning block
slides.subtitle: Reasoning logic – how should AI think?

veiksmoIntro.trumpai: **Benefit:** The Reasoning block tells AI how to think before answering. Without it – AI guesses. With it – AI justifies the answer step by step.
veiksmoIntro.darykDabar: **What to do:** Choose the Lite (3 steps) or Advanced (6 steps) template below. Copy it, add your business problem, and run AI.
veiksmoIntro.patikra: Did AI give reasoned logic, not just a conclusion? Do you see steps, assumptions, alternatives?

sections.heading: Why it works
sections.body: AI doesn't "think" on its own – it follows instructions. When you specify a reasoning sequence (CoT or ToT), the model outputs step by step or compares options. That reduces errors and improves answer logic.

slides.title: 5 Quality Control block
slides.subtitle: Quality criteria – how to check?

veiksmoIntro.trumpai: **Benefit:** Quality Control – a safety net. AI checks its own answer before giving it. Reduces hallucinations and logic gaps.
veiksmoIntro.darykDabar: **What to do:** Copy the Inline QC template below and add it to the end of your prompt. Run AI – watch for assumptions and limitations in the answer.
veiksmoIntro.patikra: Did AI mark assumptions? Did it state uncertainties? If not – strengthen the QC instruction.

sections.heading: Why it works
sections.body: When you set quality criteria (e.g. "mark assumptions", "state limitations"), AI separates what's fact-based from what's guesswork in the answer. That helps you judge the answer critically and reduces over-trust.

slides.title: 6 Advanced Parameters
slides.subtitle: Advanced parameters – how to control precisely?

veiksmoIntro.trumpai: **Benefit:** Temperature controls creativity, Reasoning – depth of thought. You can try right away: pick one example below, copy and run AI.
veiksmoIntro.darykDabar: **What to do:** Pick one of the 3 examples below (business analysis, marketing, SOP). Copy the block with ADVANCED parameters and your task, run AI. Examples below.
veiksmoIntro.patikra: Did the answer match the settings (short/long, creative/precise)? Would you change temperature or reasoning next time?

content.heroBody: Optional but powerful block: control AI predictability, depth, and creativity for your business task.

temperatureLevels.label: Low → facts, analysis, instructions
temperatureLevels.note: The lower the temperature – the less improvisation.

reasoningLevels.label: Deep → analysis, strategy, comparing options
reasoningLevels.note: Deep = more time, but better logic.

content.cheatSheetTitle: When to use what? (Business cheat sheet)

examples.label: Business analysis
examples.copyText:
ADVANCED:
Temperature: 0.2
Reasoning: Deep

Task:
Analyze this company's process and provide 3 problems and 3 realistic optimization recommendations.

examples.label: Marketing copy
examples.copyText:
ADVANCED:
Temperature: 0.7
Reasoning: Normal

Task:
Write a LinkedIn post about a new service. Tone – professional, focused on business value.

examples.label: SOP / instruction
examples.copyText:
ADVANCED:
Temperature: 0.1
Reasoning: Normal

Task:
Create a step-by-step instruction for a new employee, no interpretation.

errors: High temperature for analysis → "looks nice, but inaccurate"
Deep reasoning for simple tasks → too slow
Changing parameters but not the task formulation

sections.heading: Why it works
sections.body: Temperature and reasoning depth change how the model generates answers: lower temperature – more precise and consistent; higher – more variation. By setting parameters in the prompt or tool, you align the result better with your goal.

slides.title: 6 Advanced Parameters (II)
slides.subtitle: Response control – length, focus, repetition

veiksmoIntro.trumpai: **Benefit:** You control response length (max tokens), focus (top-p), and repetition (penalties). First – a clear task; then parameters. You can try one example below right away.
veiksmoIntro.darykDabar: **What to do:** Pick one parameter below (Max Tokens, Top-p, Frequency, or Presence). Copy the example, add your task, run AI. Examples under each parameter.
veiksmoIntro.patikra: Did response length match expectations? Fewer repetitions (or more new ideas)? If not – change the parameter value and run again.

slides.title: Full prompt example
slides.subtitle: All 6 blocks work together

practicalTask.title: Create your full prompt
practicalTask.motivation: Use it at work – create a structured prompt for a real task from your job or business.
practicalTask.inputHint: Enter the full prompt in the field below – all blocks (META, INPUT, OUTPUT, etc.) in your own words, not just placeholders.
practicalTask.placeholder:
META: You are...

INPUT: Data...

OUTPUT: Format...

REASONING: Thought sequence...

QUALITY: Criteria...

ADVANCED: Parameters...
practicalTask.explanation: Use the template above and build a full prompt for your real task. Remember: each block adds to the next and creates clear context for the AI model.

slides.title: Before and After
slides.subtitle: Structured vs unstructured prompt comparison

content.introText: What's compared: the same task, different structure (no blocks vs with blocks).
content.unstructuredPrompt: Create a training programme about AI for me. It should be interesting and practical.
content.structuredPrompt:
META: You are a training designer. Goal – prepare a 4-hour intro to AI. Audience – 12–15 beginners.
INPUT: Constraints – 1 facilitator, no live tool demos.
OUTPUT: Table with 5 columns: module, duration, goal, activity, result. Tone – clear, professional.

slides.title: Glossary
slides.subtitle: Key Module 1 terms – prompt, blocks, techniques

terms.definition: A text instruction or question you give an AI model to get the answer you want; your way of communicating with the AI system.
The art and practice of communicating effectively with AI: specification (goal, audience), structure (context, format), and iteration (testing, refinement).
A process where input is not just a question but a prompt and data; output is analysis, document, or plan, not just a reply.
Sets the AI's goal and role (e.g. "You are a marketing analyst…").
Shapes style and tone (e.g. "Imagine you're a company lead…").
A request with no examples – one question or instruction.
A request with a few examples so AI adopts the style or format.
A single step-by-step chain of reasoning; good for analysis and calculation.
Several branches or options; you pick the best; good for strategy and creativity.
Role (who you are), context and goal (what to create), audience (who for).
Data, facts, constraints – what you have before you start.
Format, structure, requirements – what you want to get (table, list, tone).
Specifies what logic AI should use before answering (CoT or ToT).
Quality criteria: sources, objectivity, limits, "don't know" rule.
Model settings: temperature, reasoning depth, response length, repetition control.

slides.title: Module summary
slides.subtitle: Main ideas and what you learned

content.introBody: Well done! You now know how to structure prompts professionally using the 6-block system, workflow, and prompting techniques.

sections.heading: 6 main blocks
items: Meta – role, context, goal (who you are and what you do)
Input – data, facts, constraints (what you have)
Output – format, structure, tone (what you want)

sections.heading: Next step
items: Now that you know the 6-block system, workflow, and techniques, it's time to check your knowledge – Module 2 quiz.

content.tagline: Structured prompts = predictable results = better efficiency
content.reflectionPrompt:
META: You are a learning reflection assistant. Goal – help consolidate knowledge after Module 1.
INPUT: I just finished training on prompt engineering – the 6-block system (Meta, Input, Output, Reasoning, Quality, Advanced) and workflow principles.
OUTPUT: Ask 3 questions: (1) Which block could I use at work today? (2) What was newest or most surprising? (3) What do I want to try first? After my answers – 1 concrete tip on how to start.

businessExamples.description: Sales reports with concrete key indicators

modules.subtitle: 15 random questions from an updated question bank.
modules.description: Five formats: multiple choice, matching, ordering, true/false, and scenario-based questions.

slides.title: Quiz intro
slides.subtitle: 15 interactive questions – 5 different formats

content.whyBenefit: After this quiz your knowledge will grow by another 60%.
content.footer: Next – slide 2: Meta and Input – Quiz

slides.title: Meta and Input – Quiz
slides.subtitle: Questions about role, context, and data

testQuestions.question: What's the best place to start a prompt?
testQuestions.explanation: The Meta block is the most important – it sets the context for the rest of the prompt.
testQuestions.hint: Think about what sets the 'who I am' and 'why I'm writing' context.

testQuestions.question: The Meta block only defines the AI's role – audience and experience are in other blocks.
testQuestions.explanation: The Meta block covers everything: WHO you are (role), WHAT experience, and WHO the result is for (audience).
testQuestions.hint: Remember: Meta = role + experience + target audience.

testQuestions.question: What is 'concrete input'?
testQuestions.explanation: Concrete input = concrete data: 250k EUR, +15%, 1200 orders.
testQuestions.hint: Input is facts, numbers, and data – not mood or questions.

content.footer: Next – slide 3: Output and Reasoning – Quiz

slides.title: Output and Reasoning – Quiz
slides.subtitle: Questions about format and reasoning sequence

testQuestions.question: You want a 5-point list in English. Which block should you specify?
testQuestions.explanation: Format (list, 5 points, language) is the Output block's job.
testQuestions.hint: Which block sets the result's format and structure?

testQuestions.question: The Reasoning block is only needed for complex questions – for simple ones Meta + Input + Output is enough.
testQuestions.explanation: Correct! Minimal prompt = Meta + Input + Output (3 blocks). Add Reasoning when you need more complex thinking (e.g. analyse, compare, recommend).
testQuestions.hint: Think: does a simple 'Translate this sentence' need a reasoning sequence?

testQuestions.question: What is the Reasoning block for?
options: To specify how AI should think before answering
testQuestions.explanation: The Reasoning block specifies the THOUGHT SEQUENCE: 1) Analyse 2) Compare 3) Recommend.
testQuestions.hint: Reasoning = thought sequence. Think about steps before the answer.

slides.title: 6-block system – Match pairs
slides.subtitle: Match each block to its function

testQuestions.question: Match each block to its main function:
testQuestions.explanation: Each of the 6 blocks has a clear responsibility. When they work together – the result is predictable and good quality.
testQuestions.hint: Think: which block answers 'who am I?', 'what do I have?', 'what do I want?', 'how to think?', 'is it good?', 'what settings?'

slides.title: Priority ordering
slides.subtitle: Order the blocks by priority

testQuestions.question: Order the 6 blocks from most to least important (by priority):
testQuestions.explanation: Meta, Input, Output – required (top 3). Reasoning, Quality, Advanced – optional but important for harder tasks.
testQuestions.hint: The first three blocks are the 'required minimum' for a clear result.

content.footer: Next – slide 6: Advanced and Quality – Quiz

slides.title: Advanced and Quality – Quiz
slides.subtitle: Questions about parameters and quality control

testQuestions.question: You want a more creative answer. What's best to change?
testQuestions.explanation: Temperature 0.8–1.0 = more creative. Temperature 0.1–0.3 = more precise, factual.
testQuestions.hint: Which parameter controls creativity vs precision?

testQuestions.question: The Quality Control block only checks the result after AI has already generated the answer.
testQuestions.explanation: We set Quality Control criteria before generation – it's an instruction to AI to 'check yourself': is it logical? Are facts accurate? Is it complete?
testQuestions.hint: Think: is Quality Control an instruction to AI, or an action after the result?

testQuestions.question: How many blocks does a full structured prompt have?
testQuestions.explanation: 6 blocks: Meta, Input, Output, Reasoning, Quality, Advanced.

slides.title: Business scenario – Quiz
slides.subtitle: Apply your knowledge in a real scenario

testQuestions.scenarioContext: You're a marketing lead. You're writing a prompt to AI to generate a quarterly sales report for the board. You have: 250k EUR revenue, 1200 orders, +15% growth. The prompt starts: 'You are a senior business analyst...' and ends: 'Present the result in a table with 5 columns.'
testQuestions.question: Which block is missing from this prompt?
options: Reasoning – how to analyse the data is not specified
testQuestions.explanation: The prompt has Meta (role: analyst), Input (data: 250k EUR), and Output (table, 5 columns), but no Reasoning – how should AI analyse: compare periods? Look for trends? Recommend actions?
testQuestions.hint: Compare: what's already there (role, data, format) and what's missing from the 6 blocks.

testQuestions.scenarioContext: A colleague wrote the prompt: 'Write me an article about AI.' The result – 3 pages of generic text, no structure, no sources, in English (though they wanted another language).
testQuestions.question: What would improve this prompt the most?
options: Add Meta (role: journalist), Output (1000 words, target language, with sources), and Quality (factual accuracy)
testQuestions.explanation: Bad result = unstructured prompt. Adding Meta (who writes), Output (format, language), and Quality (sources, accuracy) makes the result predictable.
testQuestions.hint: Think: how many 6-block elements are missing from this prompt?

content.footer: Next – slide 8: Workflow and techniques – Quiz

slides.title: Workflow and techniques – Quiz
slides.subtitle: Questions about workflow and techniques

testQuestions.question: How does basic chat differ from workflow?
options: Workflow has a clear format and result
testQuestions.explanation: Workflow defines format, requirements, and expected result.
testQuestions.hint: Workflow = structured process with a clear end result.

testQuestions.question: Which technique relies on examples?
testQuestions.explanation: Few-shot gives 1–2 examples, then asks for new ones. Zero-shot – no examples.
testQuestions.hint: 'Few' means 'a few' – a technique that uses several examples.

slides.title: Quiz results
slides.subtitle: Your knowledge assessment

content.footer: Next – slide 10: Bonus: Using style in prompts

slides.title: Bonus: Using style in prompts
slides.subtitle: Tone, style, audience, language – business tone, formal, compact

content.whyBenefit: You passed the knowledge check – here's a bonus: a skill few have. Tone, style, audience – in one sentence, predictable result.

sections.heading: What will make your prompts stand out
sections.body: This isn't theory – it's business practice. **Tone:** formal, friendly, business, academic (e.g. "business tone"). **Style:** concise, narrative, bullet list, compact. **Audience:** experts, beginners, leads, customers. **Language:** target language, term level ("no jargon"). **Structure:** "5 points", "max 1 page", "max 150 words".

sections.heading: How to specify in the prompt
sections.body: Clear, concrete words – not "write well", but "business tone", "formal style", "target audience: leads", "English", "bullet list with headings".

sections.heading: Examples – copy and adapt
sections.body: Professional style, formal summary, compact style, or formatting – pick one and paste into your prompt.

sections.heading: Professional style
sections.body: Copy the prompt below and use it in your AI – target audience and style will be clearly specified.
sections.copyable: Style: professional, clear, no fluff. Target audience: [Enter audience].

sections.heading: Formal summary
sections.body: Copy the prompt below and paste into AI – you'll get a formal summary (5–7 sentences).
sections.copyable: Formal tone. Provide a 5–7 sentence summary.

sections.heading: Compact style
sections.body: Copy the prompt below – AI will keep to roughly 500 words (or your chosen length).
sections.copyable: Compact style; keep to ~500 words (or max_tokens≈750).

sections.heading: Formatting
sections.body: Copy the prompt below and adapt to your format (e.g. Markdown or company style).
sections.copyable: Fix formatting to match [Markdown / company style].

sections.heading: Want more detail? Link to the 6 blocks
sections.body: **Output** – what output we want (format, structure). **Quality** – quality criteria (clarity, fit for audience, tone).

slides.title: Bonus: Practical tasks
slides.subtitle: Style texts and email replies

content.whyBenefit: Second bonus: practice. Each prompt is ready – just copy and run.

sections.heading: 1. Creating texts in different styles and formats
sections.body: Official invitation, formal agreement, enthusiastic announcement – three different styles. Copy and adapt.

sections.heading: Official invitation
sections.body: (official / formal style)
sections.copyable:
META: You are a business communications specialist. Goal – prepare an invitation.
INPUT: Event – [your business event]. Date – [next month]. Audience – partners and clients.
OUTPUT: Official invitation text, 1 page, formal tone.

sections.heading: Formal agreement
sections.body: (formal / legal style)
sections.copyable:
META: You are a legal specialist. Goal – draft an agreement.
INPUT: Product – [enter product]. Parties – seller and client. Constraints – 1–2 pages.
OUTPUT: Formal agreement with sections: parties, subject, price, terms. Legal tone.

sections.heading: Job posting
sections.body: (enthusiastic / inclusive style)
sections.copyable:
META: You are an HR specialist. Goal – attract candidates.
INPUT: Position – [enter position]. Company – [enter company]. Benefits – flexible schedule, team.
OUTPUT: Job posting, 150–200 words, enthusiastic and engaging tone.

sections.heading: 2. Replies to customer emails
sections.body: Polite, professional, respectful – three different tones. Copy and adapt.

sections.heading: Apology reply
sections.body: (polite, apologetic, problem-solving)
sections.copyable:
META: You are a customer service specialist. Goal – retain the customer.
INPUT: Situation – customer complains about late delivery. Tone – polite, apologetic.
OUTPUT: Email reply, 80–120 words, with a concrete solution and apology.

sections.heading: Informative reply
sections.body: (professional, informative)
sections.copyable:
META: You are a sales manager. Goal – provide information and encourage purchase.
INPUT: Customer asks about service prices and discounts. Services – [enter services].
OUTPUT: Professional reply, 100–150 words, with pricing structure and call to action.

sections.heading: Returns policy
sections.body: (respectful, clearly describing policy)
sections.copyable:
META: You are an e-commerce customer service specialist. Goal – clearly explain the policy.
INPUT: Customer asks about returns policy. Store – [enter store]. Deadline – 14 days.
OUTPUT: Respectful reply, 80–120 words, with return steps and conditions.

sections.heading: Adapt to your business
sections.body: Copy any prompt and run it in AI – you'll get text in the chosen style. Then change [enter product] or context to fit your needs.

businessExamples.title: 6-block system and workflow
businessExamples.description: Questions on Meta, Input, Output, Reasoning, Quality, Advanced

modules.title: Practical application
modules.subtitle: 6 business scenarios with 6 blocks
modules.description: 6 different scenarios – create your prompts and build a template library.

slides.title: Practice intro
slides.subtitle: How to do the tasks – Module 3, slide 1

content.whyBenefit: After the practice part you'll have 6 ready-made templates for everyday work.
content.optionalInstruction: Choose at least 2 scenarios that match your role or interest – you can skip the rest.

slides.title: Warm-up
slides.subtitle: A simple first practical example – 2–3 min before the scenarios

content.whyBenefit: In 2–3 minutes you'll see how even a short structured prompt gives a concrete result – without complex context.

sections.heading: In short
sections.body: In 2–3 minutes copy the prompt and run AI – you'll get 3 concrete goals for this week. This shows how META + INPUT + OUTPUT already improve the result.

sections.heading: Do it now
sections.body: Copy the prompt (below) and paste it into any AI tool – ChatGPT, Claude, or Copilot. In 1 minute you'll get 3 business goals for this week.
sections.copyable:
META: You are a business assistant. Your goal is to help formulate 3 concrete goals for this week.

INPUT: My context – [enter yours: e.g. client work, project deadlines, sales].

OUTPUT: Give 3 numbered goals for this week. Each goal – 1–2 sentences, concrete and achievable.

sections.heading: Check
sections.body: If you got 3 concrete goals – great. If the answer is too vague – add your role or area in the INPUT block.

content.footer: Next – slide 3: Scenario 1: Executive strategy report

slides.title: Scenario 1: Executive strategy report
slides.subtitle: Quarterly or half-year results overview for the board

scenario.situation: You're a lead and need to prepare a quarterly results overview for the board in a few days. The board will decide on priorities for the next quarters, so you need to clearly summarise numbers, risks, and suggest concrete steps.
scenario.context: You're a lead. You need to prepare a quarterly or half-year results overview for the board or owners.
scenario.data: Q2 2024: revenue 1.2M EUR (+8% vs Q1), margin 23%. Project "X" status: 70% done, risk – 2-week delay. Top 3 Q3 priorities: sales growth, company restructuring, new CRM.
scenario.constraints: Report due in 5 days. Board has 4 members. Max length: 2 pages.
scenario.expectedFormat: Executive Summary (1–2 pages), KPI dashboard, 3 priorities with recommendations

practicalTask.title: Create a full prompt for an executive strategy report
practicalTask.placeholder:
META: You are...

INPUT: Data...

OUTPUT: Format...

REASONING: Thought sequence...

QUALITY: Criteria...

ADVANCED: Parameters...
practicalTask.template:
META:
You are a senior executive with 10+ years of experience in strategic decisions. Your goal is to prepare a quarterly results overview for the board, who will decide on Q3 priorities.

INPUT:
Q2 2024: revenue 1.2M EUR (+8% vs Q1), margin 23%. Project "X" status: 70% done, risk – 2-week delay. Top 3 Q3 priorities: sales growth, restructuring, new CRM.

Constraints: 5 days, 4 board members, max 2 pages.

OUTPUT:
Format: Executive Summary (1–2 pages). Structure: 1) KPI dashboard, 2) Risks and project status, 3) 3 priorities with recommendations. Language: English, business tone.

REASONING (CoT):
1) Summarise KPI and trends
2) Assess risks and project status
3) Prioritise and formulate recommendations

QUALITY:
✓ Numbers accurate ✓ Risks identified ✓ Recommendations clear ✓ Length 1–2 pages

ADVANCED:
Temperature: 0.3 (factual tone), Format: business document
practicalTask.explanation: A strategy report requires decision logic, summaries, and clear recommendations.

steps.title: META block – Start with role
steps.description: Define who you are and who the document is for
steps.hint: Role: executive. Goal: strategy report. Audience: board.
steps.partialSolution:
META:
You are a senior executive with 10+ years of experience in strategic decisions. Your goal is to prepare a quarterly results overview for the board.

steps.title: INPUT block – Gather data
steps.hint: Data: revenue, margin, project status, priorities. Constraints: 5 days, 4 members.
steps.partialSolution:
INPUT:
Q2 2024: revenue 1.2M EUR (+8% vs Q1), margin 23%. Project "X" status: 70% done, risk – 2-week delay. Top 3 Q3 priorities: sales growth, restructuring, new CRM.

Constraints: 5 days, 4 board members, max 2 pages.

steps.title: OUTPUT block – Define format
steps.description: Specify the structure and KPI dashboard
steps.hint: Format: Executive Summary. Structure: KPI, risks, 3 priorities.
steps.partialSolution:
OUTPUT:
Format: Executive Summary (1–2 pages). Structure: 1) KPI dashboard, 2) Risks and project status, 3) 3 priorities with recommendations. Language: English, business tone.

steps.title: REASONING block – Set thought sequence
steps.hint: CoT: 1) KPI summary, 2) Risk assessment, 3) Priority recommendations.
steps.partialSolution:
REASONING (CoT):
1) Summarise KPI and trends
2) Assess risks and project status
3) Prioritise and formulate recommendations

steps.title: QUALITY block – Set quality criteria
steps.hint: Numbers accurate, risks identified, recommendations clear.
steps.partialSolution:
QUALITY:
✓ Numbers accurate ✓ Risks identified ✓ Recommendations clear ✓ Length 1–2 pages

steps.title: ADVANCED block – Set parameters
steps.hint: Temperature: 0.3 (factual tone).
steps.partialSolution:
ADVANCED:
Temperature: 0.3 (factual tone), Format: business document

content.footer: Next – slide 4: Scenario 2: Sales analysis and action plan

slides.title: Scenario 2: Sales analysis and action plan
slides.subtitle: Numbers → insights → actions

scenario.situation: Sales didn't meet plan and leadership wants to understand why quickly and get a concrete action list. You have data by segment and period – you need to turn it into insights and recommendations with owners and deadlines.
scenario.context: Sales growth, decline, or stagnation. You need to analyse the data and propose concrete actions to improve results.
scenario.data: Q3 2024: 320k EUR revenue (plan 350k, -8.6%). 1450 orders. Segments: B2B (38%), B2C (62%). B2B down -12%, B2C +3%. Average order: B2B 890 EUR, B2C 165 EUR.
scenario.constraints: Analysis in 1 week. Team: 2 analysts. Leadership wants 5 concrete actions with deadlines and owners.
scenario.expectedFormat: Analysis + 5 concrete actions with deadlines and owners

practicalTask.title: Create a full prompt for sales analysis and action plan
practicalTask.placeholder:
META: You are...

INPUT: Data...

OUTPUT: Format...

REASONING: Thought sequence...

QUALITY: Criteria...

ADVANCED: Parameters...
practicalTask.explanation: Sales analysis needs number interpretation and concrete actions with deadlines.

steps.title: META block – Start with role
steps.description: Define who you are and who the document is for
steps.hint: Role: marketing lead. Goal: Q1 2025 marketing plan. Audience: CEO.
steps.partialSolution:
META:
You are a marketing lead with 10+ years of B2B experience. Your goal is to prepare the Q1 2025 strategic marketing plan for the CEO, who will make budget decisions.

steps.title: INPUT block – Gather data
steps.description: List concrete numbers, metrics, and constraints
steps.hint: Data: Q4 results, CAC, conversion, competitors. Constraints: 2 weeks, 3 people, B2B priority.
steps.partialSolution:
INPUT:
Q4 2024 results: 45 new customers, CAC 6500 EUR, conversion 2.8%. Competitor growth: +25% and +18%. Planned Q1 budget: 300k EUR.

Constraints: Plan due in 2 weeks. Team: 3 people. Priority – B2B segment.

steps.title: OUTPUT block – Define format
steps.hint: Format: strategic document. Structure: action plan + KPI. Language: English, business tone.
steps.partialSolution:
OUTPUT:
Format: Strategic document (5–7 pages). Structure: 1) Situation analysis, 2) Q1 goals and KPI, 3) Action plan by channel, 4) Budget allocation, 5) Risks and assumptions. Language: English, professional business tone.

steps.title: REASONING block – Set thought sequence
steps.description: Specify how AI should think before creating the plan
steps.hint: Use CoT (Chain of Thought) – one logical sequence. 1) Analyse Q4 results, 2) Assess competitor threat, 3) Set Q1 goals, 4) Formulate strategy.
steps.partialSolution:
REASONING (CoT):
1) Analyse Q4 2024 results and identify strengths/weaknesses
2) Assess competitor growth threat and market trends
3) Set Q1 2025 goals by budget (300k EUR) and B2B priority
4) Formulate a concrete action plan with KPI and budget allocation

steps.title: QUALITY block – Set quality criteria
steps.description: Define how to check result quality
steps.hint: Check: concrete KPI, budget allocation, B2B focus, feasibility.
steps.partialSolution:
QUALITY:
✓ All KPI concrete and measurable
✓ Budget (300k EUR) allocated by channel
✓ Plan focused on B2B segment
✓ Actions doable in 2 weeks with 3 people
✓ Document suitable for CEO (strategic level)

steps.title: ADVANCED block – Set parameters
steps.hint: Temperature: 0.4–0.5 (strategic but creative). Reasoning: normal or extended.
steps.partialSolution:
ADVANCED:
Temperature: 0.4 (strategic thinking with creativity)
Reasoning: extended (deeper analysis needed)
Format: structured business document

practicalTask.template:
META:
You are a marketing lead with 10+ years of B2B experience. Your goal is to prepare the Q1 2025 strategic marketing plan for the CEO, who will make budget decisions.

INPUT:
Q4 2024 results: 45 new customers, CAC 6500 EUR, conversion 2.8%. Competitor growth: +25% and +18%. Planned Q1 budget: 300k EUR.

Constraints: Plan due in 2 weeks. Team: 3 people. Priority – B2B segment.

OUTPUT:
Format: Strategic document (5–7 pages). Structure: 1) Situation analysis, 2) Q1 goals and KPI, 3) Action plan by channel, 4) Budget allocation, 5) Risks and assumptions. Language: English, professional business tone.

REASONING (CoT):
1) Analyse Q4 2024 results and identify strengths/weaknesses
2) Assess competitor growth threat and market trends
3) Set Q1 2025 goals by budget (300k EUR) and B2B priority
4) Formulate a concrete action plan with KPI and budget allocation

QUALITY:
✓ All KPI concrete and measurable
✓ Budget (300k EUR) allocated by channel
✓ Plan focused on B2B segment
✓ Actions doable in 2 weeks with 3 people
✓ Document suitable for CEO (strategic level)

ADVANCED:
Temperature: 0.4 (strategic thinking with creativity)
Reasoning: extended (deeper analysis needed)
Format: structured business document

content.footer: Next – slide 5: Scenario 3: Marketing campaign plan

slides.title: Scenario 3: Marketing campaign plan
slides.subtitle: Audience, channels, KPI

scenario.situation: You're planning a new campaign or product launch and have a limited budget and timeline. You need to spell out: who the campaign is for, which channels will reach them, and how you'll measure success.
scenario.context: New campaign or product communication in the market. You need a clear action plan with audience, channels, and KPI.
scenario.data: Campaign: new product "SmartFlow". Budget: 45k EUR. Target audience: IT leads (companies with 100–500 employees). Channels: LinkedIn, events, email. Competitors: 2 main. Period: Q2 2025.
scenario.constraints: Plan in 2 weeks. Team: 2 people. Need KPI and measurement plan.
scenario.expectedFormat: Strategic document with channels, KPI, timeline

practicalTask.title: Create a full prompt for a marketing campaign plan
practicalTask.placeholder:
META: You are...

INPUT: Data...

OUTPUT: Format...

REASONING: Thought sequence...

QUALITY: Criteria...

ADVANCED: Parameters...
practicalTask.explanation: For a marketing campaign, focus on: audience, channels, KPI, and how you'll measure results.

steps.title: META block – Start with role
steps.description: Define who you are and who the document is for
steps.hint: Role: HR lead. Goal: survey analysis and recommendations. Audience: leadership.
steps.partialSolution:
META:
You are an HR lead with 8+ years in employee experience. Your goal is to analyse the annual staff survey results and give concrete recommendations to leadership, who will decide on the action plan.

steps.title: INPUT block – Gather data
steps.description: List concrete numbers, metrics, and constraints
steps.hint: Data: participation, satisfaction, problem areas, strengths. Constraints: budget 50k EUR, concrete action plan.
steps.partialSolution:
INPUT:
156/180 employees participated (87%). Overall satisfaction: 7.2/10 (+0.4 vs last year). Problem areas: career opportunities (5.8), work-life balance (6.1). Strengths: teamwork (8.4), manager support (7.9).

Constraints: Leadership wants a concrete action plan. Budget for initiatives: 50k EUR. Confidentiality: do not disclose individual responses.

steps.title: OUTPUT block – Define format
steps.hint: Format: report. Structure: analysis + visuals + action plan. Language: English, sensitive tone.
steps.partialSolution:
OUTPUT:
Format: Report with visuals (6–8 pages). Structure: 1) Executive summary, 2) Problem area analysis, 3) Strengths, 4) Concrete action plan with budget allocation, 5) Expected outcomes. Language: English, professional but sensitive tone.

steps.title: REASONING block – Set thought sequence
steps.description: Specify how AI should think before creating the analysis
steps.hint: Use CoT (Chain of Thought). 1) Analyse problem areas, 2) Assess causes, 3) Formulate solutions, 4) Allocate budget.
steps.partialSolution:
REASONING (CoT):
1) Analyse problem areas (career 5.8, work-life balance 6.1) and their causes
2) Assess why strengths work (teamwork 8.4, manager support 7.9)
3) Formulate concrete, actionable solutions for each problem area
4) Allocate budget (50k EUR) by priority and expected impact

steps.title: QUALITY block – Set quality criteria
steps.description: Define how to check result quality
steps.hint: Check: sensitivity to data, concrete actions, budget allocation, feasibility.
steps.partialSolution:
QUALITY:
✓ Sensitive handling of employee data (confidentiality)
✓ Concrete, actionable steps per problem area
✓ Budget (50k EUR) allocated by priority
✓ Action plan has clear deadlines and owners
✓ Document suitable for leadership (strategic level with concrete actions)

steps.title: ADVANCED block – Set parameters
steps.hint: Temperature: 0.3–0.4 (sensitive but professional). Reasoning: extended (deeper analysis).
steps.partialSolution:
ADVANCED:
Temperature: 0.3 (sensitive, professional tone)
Reasoning: extended (deeper analysis and empathy)
Format: structured HR document with visuals

practicalTask.template:
META:
You are an HR lead with 8+ years in employee experience. Your goal is to analyse the annual staff survey results and give concrete recommendations to leadership, who will decide on the action plan.

INPUT:
156/180 employees participated (87%). Overall satisfaction: 7.2/10 (+0.4 vs last year). Problem areas: career opportunities (5.8), work-life balance (6.1). Strengths: teamwork (8.4), manager support (7.9).

Constraints: Leadership wants a concrete action plan. Budget for initiatives: 50k EUR. Confidentiality: do not disclose individual responses.

OUTPUT:
Format: Report with visuals (6–8 pages). Structure: 1) Executive summary, 2) Problem area analysis, 3) Strengths, 4) Concrete action plan with budget allocation, 5) Expected outcomes. Language: English, professional but sensitive tone.

REASONING (CoT):
1) Analyse problem areas (career 5.8, work-life balance 6.1) and their causes
2) Assess why strengths work (teamwork 8.4, manager support 7.9)
3) Formulate concrete, actionable solutions per problem area
4) Allocate budget (50k EUR) by priority and expected impact

QUALITY:
✓ Sensitive handling of employee data (confidentiality)
✓ Concrete, actionable steps per problem area
✓ Budget (50k EUR) allocated by priority
✓ Action plan has clear deadlines and owners
✓ Document suitable for leadership (strategic level with concrete actions)

ADVANCED:
Temperature: 0.3 (sensitive, professional tone)
Reasoning: extended (deeper analysis and empathy)
Format: structured HR document with visuals

content.footer: Next – slide 6: Scenario 4: Internal comms document

slides.title: Scenario 4: Internal comms document
slides.subtitle: Change message to staff

scenario.situation: Big changes are happening – a new system rollout or restructuring – and staff are worried. Your job is to draft a message that clearly explains what's changing, why, what to do next, and answers the most common questions.
scenario.context: Organisational change (strategy, restructuring, new system). You need a clear message staff can understand.
scenario.data: Company is moving to a new CRM. Changes start in 6 weeks. All 120 staff will have training. Most common question: "Will we lose our jobs?"
scenario.constraints: Message in 3 days. Max 500 words. Tone: caring, clear. Include a Q&A section.
scenario.expectedFormat: Internal message / email, 300–500 words, Q&A section

practicalTask.title: Create a full prompt for an internal comms document
practicalTask.placeholder:
META: You are...

INPUT: Data...

OUTPUT: Format...

REASONING: Thought sequence...

QUALITY: Criteria...

ADVANCED: Parameters...
practicalTask.explanation: For internal comms, focus on: tone, clarity, reducing resistance, and a Q&A section.

steps.title: META block – Start with role
steps.description: Define who you are and who the message is for
steps.hint: Role: internal comms lead. Goal: message to staff. Audience: all employees.
steps.partialSolution:
META:
You are an internal comms lead. Your goal is to prepare a clear message to staff about changes in the organisation, reducing worry and resistance.

steps.title: INPUT block – Gather data
steps.description: List: what's changing, why, when, top questions
steps.hint: Data: CRM change, 6 weeks, 120 staff, training. Question: "Will we lose our jobs?"
steps.partialSolution:
INPUT:
Company moving to new CRM. Changes in 6 weeks. 120 staff – training. Most common question: "Will we lose our jobs?" Constraints: 3 days, max 500 words, caring tone.

steps.title: OUTPUT block – Define format
steps.hint: Format: email 300–500 words. Structure: what's changing, why, what to do next, Q&A.
steps.partialSolution:
OUTPUT:
Format: Internal message (300–500 words). Structure: 1) What's changing, 2) Why, 3) When and what to do, 4) Q&A section. Tone: caring, clear.

steps.title: REASONING block – Set thought sequence
steps.hint: CoT: 1) Explain clearly, 2) Address fears, 3) What staff need to know.
steps.partialSolution:
REASONING (CoT):
1) Explain the changes as clearly as possible
2) Address fears ("Will we lose our jobs?" etc.)
3) Give concrete next steps and Q&A

steps.title: QUALITY block – Set quality criteria
steps.hint: Caring tone, clarity, Q&A section, length 300–500 words.
steps.partialSolution:
QUALITY:
✓ Caring, clear tone ✓ Q&A section included ✓ Length 300–500 words ✓ Reduces worry

steps.title: ADVANCED block – Set parameters
steps.hint: Temperature: 0.4 (empathetic but professional).
steps.partialSolution:
ADVANCED:
Temperature: 0.4 (empathetic tone), Format: internal message

practicalTask.template:
META:
You are an internal comms lead. Your goal is to prepare a clear message to staff about the CRM change, reducing worry.

INPUT:
Company moving to new CRM. Changes in 6 weeks. 120 staff – training. Most common question: "Will we lose our jobs?" Constraints: 3 days, max 500 words.

OUTPUT:
Format: Internal message (300–500 words). Structure: 1) What's changing, 2) Why, 3) When and what to do, 4) Q&A. Tone: caring, clear.

REASONING: 1) Explain clearly, 2) Address fears, 3) Q&A section.

QUALITY: ✓ Caring tone ✓ Q&A included ✓ 300–500 words

ADVANCED: Temperature: 0.4, Format: internal message

scenario.context: Staff survey results, motivation or effectiveness. You need to analyse the situation and propose an action plan to leadership.
scenario.data: 156/180 employees participated (87%). Overall satisfaction: 7.2/10 (+0.4 vs last year). Problem areas: career opportunities (5.8), work-life balance (6.1). Strengths: teamwork (8.4), manager support (7.9). Budget for initiatives: 50k EUR.
scenario.constraints: Leadership wants a concrete action plan. Confidentiality: do not disclose individual responses.
scenario.expectedFormat: Report with analysis and action plan

content.footer: Next – slide 8: Scenario 6: Customer complaint handling

slides.title: Scenario 6: Customer complaint handling
slides.subtitle: Reply to customer and internal action plan

scenario.situation: A customer is complaining about delay and unclear communication – there's reputational risk and you need to respond quickly. You have to prepare a two-part response: an empathetic reply to the customer and an internal plan for the team – what to change so it doesn't happen again.
scenario.context: Unhappy customer or reputational risk. You need a reply to the customer and an internal action plan to fix the issue.
scenario.data: Customer complains: 2-week delay, unclear communication. Customer: B2B, long-standing (3 years). Contacted by email. Reputational risk: may leave a review.
scenario.constraints: Reply within 24 hours. Tone: empathetic, accountable. Need: reply to customer + internal action plan (3–5 points).
scenario.expectedFormat: Reply to customer (email) + internal action plan (3–5 points)

practicalTask.title: Create a full prompt for customer complaint handling
practicalTask.placeholder:
META: You are...

INPUT: Data...

OUTPUT: Format...

REASONING: Thought sequence...

QUALITY: Criteria...

ADVANCED: Parameters...
practicalTask.explanation: Complaint handling needs empathy, accountability, and a clear structure for the response.

steps.title: META block – Start with role
steps.description: Define who you are and who it's for
steps.hint: Role: customer service lead. Goal: reply + internal plan.
steps.partialSolution:
META:
You are a customer service lead. Your goal is to prepare an empathetic reply to the customer and an internal action plan to fix the issue.

steps.title: INPUT block – Gather data
steps.hint: Data: delay, communication, B2B, 3 years, reputational risk.
steps.partialSolution:
INPUT:
Complaint: 2-week delay, unclear communication. Customer: B2B, long-standing (3 years). Reputational risk. Constraints: 24 hours, empathetic tone.

steps.title: OUTPUT block – Define format
steps.hint: Format: email to customer + 3–5 point internal plan.
steps.partialSolution:
OUTPUT:
Format: 1) Email to customer (empathetic, accountable), 2) Internal plan 3–5 points. Language: English.

steps.title: REASONING block – Set thought sequence
steps.hint: CoT: 1) Acknowledge the problem, 2) Offer solution, 3) Internal plan.
steps.partialSolution:
REASONING (CoT):
1) Acknowledge the problem and respond with empathy
2) Give the customer a concrete solution
3) Formulate internal action plan (3–5 points)

steps.title: QUALITY block – Set quality criteria
steps.hint: Empathy, accountability, concrete actions.
steps.partialSolution:
QUALITY:
✓ Empathetic tone ✓ Accountability acknowledged ✓ Concrete actions ✓ Internal plan 3–5 points

steps.title: ADVANCED block – Set parameters
steps.hint: Temperature: 0.4 (empathetic but controlled).
steps.partialSolution:
ADVANCED:
Temperature: 0.4, Format: customer service document

META:
You are a customer service lead. Your goal is to prepare an empathetic reply to the customer and an internal action plan.

INPUT:
Complaint: 2-week delay, unclear communication. Customer: B2B, 3 years. Reputational risk. Constraints: 24 hours, empathetic tone.

OUTPUT:
Format: 1) Email to customer, 2) Internal plan 3–5 points. Language: English.

REASONING: 1) Acknowledge problem, 2) Offer solution, 3) Internal plan.

QUALITY: ✓ Empathy ✓ Accountability ✓ Concrete actions

ADVANCED: Temperature: 0.4, Format: customer service

slides.title: Practice summary
slides.subtitle: What you learned and next steps

content.introBody: Well done! You applied the 6-block system in 6 business scenarios – you created structured prompts for real work: board reports, sales analysis, marketing plans, internal comms, people decisions, and customer complaint handling.

sections.heading: 6 business scenarios
items: Marketing plan – audience, channels, key indicators
Internal comms – change message to staff
People analysis – survey results and action plan
Customer complaint handling – reply and internal plan

sections.heading: 6-block application
items: Meta – role and context for the scenario

sections.heading: Main ideas
items: Context → data → format in each scenario
CoT thought sequence – priorities and logic
Template library – your prompts for work

sections.heading: Next step
items: You've finished the 3-module programme. You can move on to Module 4: Context engineering (RAG, Deep research, tokens) or start using your templates at work.
Check the prompt library – ready-made templates by topic (Prompt library section on the main page).

content.reflectionPrompt:
META: You are a learning reflection assistant. Goal – help consolidate practical knowledge after Module 3.
INPUT: I just finished the practice module – I applied the 6-block system in 6 business scenarios (board report, sales analysis, marketing plan, internal comms, people analysis, complaint handling).
OUTPUT: Ask 3 questions: (1) Which scenario could I use at work today? (2) What was newest in the practice? (3) What do I want to try with my real data? After my answers – 1 concrete tip on how to start.
content.tagline: 6 blocks + 6 scenarios = your template library for the workday

slides.title: Part 1 summary
slides.subtitle: What you learned – 3 modules: 6 blocks, quiz, and practice

content.introBody: Well done! You've completed Part 1: you learned the 6-block system (Module 1), checked your knowledge in the quiz (Module 2), and applied prompts in 6 business scenarios (Module 3). You now have a solid base for further learning or for use at work.

sections.heading: Module 1 – 6-block system
items: Meta, Input, Output, Reasoning, Quality, Advanced
Workflow and techniques (Zero-shot, Few-shot, CoT, ToT)

sections.heading: Module 3 – Practice
items: 6 business scenarios: board, sales, marketing, comms, people, complaints

sections.heading: Where to apply?
items: You can use the 6-block system and structured prompts every day: short replies, reports, emails, meeting summaries, strategy points.
From Module 4 onwards – sources and RAG; your foundation is already in place.

sections.heading: Next step
items: Go to Module 4: Context engineering (RAG, Deep research, tokens, manipulation, hallucinations) – or start using your templates at work.

content.reflectionPrompt:
META: You are a learning reflection assistant. Goal – help consolidate knowledge after Part 1.
INPUT: I just finished 3 modules: 6-block system (Meta, Input, Output, Reasoning, Quality, Advanced), knowledge quiz, and 6 practice scenarios.
OUTPUT: Ask 3 questions: (1) What can I apply at work today? (2) What was newest or most surprising? (3) What do I want to try first? After my answers – 1 concrete tip on how to start.
content.tagline: 3 modules = 6 blocks + quiz + 6 scenarios – your foundation for what's next.
